original_hypothesis.title: WebAuthn authenticatorData Minimum Length Not Validated Before Flag Access
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify accesses webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] to check UP and UV flags without first verifying that authenticatorData has at least 33 bytes. If authenticatorData is shorter than 33 bytes, the access causes an out-of-bounds revert rather than a graceful false return. This forces _validateSignature to revert instead of returning false, which has different semantic meaning in ERC-4337 validation.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: In validateUserOp, _validateSignature is expected to return true/false (0/1 validation result). A revert instead causes the entire validateUserOp to revert, making the UserOp unbundleable rather than just invalid. This could be used to cause permanent gas waste for bundlers if authenticatorData can be manipulated, though the authenticator controls this data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] accessed without length >= 33 check; out-of-bounds revert instead of returning false

preconditions: WebAuthnAuth struct contains authenticatorData shorter than 33 bytes
This struct is submitted as part of a UserOp signature or ERC-1271 call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Direct array index access without bounds check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: authenticatorData[32] reverts on short data instead of returning false; inconsistent with other validation checks that return false

analysis_questions: Can an attacker craft a UserOp with short authenticatorData that causes revert in validateUserOp?
Does the revert propagate differently than returning false/1 in ERC-4337 context?
Could this be used to grief bundlers by wasting their gas?
validation_requirements: Test WebAuthn.verify with authenticatorData of length 0, 1, 32
Verify the revert propagation through _validateSignature and validateUserOp
Compare bundler behavior for revert vs sigFailed=1 return

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits a UserOp with a WebAuthnAuth signature containing truncated authenticatorData (< 33 bytes). validateUserOp reverts instead of returning 1 (sig failure). Bundler wastes gas on simulation that reverts. Repeated submission could grief bundlers processing this wallet's UserOps.
prerequisites: Attacker can submit UserOps to bundler

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bundler gas waste; potential temporary denial of service for the wallet
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Bundlers processing UserOps for the wallet; wallet usability
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Full — legitimate UserOps with proper authenticatorData still work

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for short authenticatorData edge case

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Revert in validateUserOp visible to bundler

validation_result.mechanism: missing bounds check on authenticatorData before index access
validation_result.impact_summary: WebAuthn.verify reverts on short authenticatorData instead of returning false, causing validateUserOp and isValidSignature to revert rather than gracefully indicate signature failure.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The WebAuthn.verify function accesses webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] at two points (UP flag check and UV flag check) without first verifying that authenticatorData.length >= 33. In Solidity 0.8+, out-of-bounds array access triggers a Panic(0x32) revert. This means malformed WebAuthn signatures with short authenticatorData cause a hard revert rather than a graceful false return. This is inconsistent with the other validation checks in the same function (malleability, type, challenge) which all return false on failure. In the ERC-4337 context, validateUserOp is expected to return 1 for signature failures per the spec comment in CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol, not revert. The revert propagates through _validateSignature -> validateUserOp, changing the semantic meaning of the failure. For ERC-1271 isValidSignature, it should return 0xffffffff instead of reverting.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies WebAuthn.sol lines around 141-148. The authenticatorData[32] access occurs without any preceding length validation. All other validation checks in verify() (lines ~126-140) return false on failure, but this access causes a revert. The authenticatorData is user-supplied via the signature field in UserOp, making this reachable by any external party.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Direct index access at position 32 without checking authenticatorData.length >= 33. If authenticatorData is shorter than 33 bytes, this triggers a Solidity Panic(0x32) revert instead of returning false like all other validation checks in this function.

calling_sequence: 3. _validateSignature decodes SignatureWrapper from signature, loads ownerBytes
4. For 64-byte owner (passkey): decodes WebAuthnAuth from sigWrapper.signatureData
5. Calls WebAuthn.verify({challenge: abi.encode(message), requireUV: false, webAuthnAuth: auth, x: x, y: y})
6. WebAuthn.verify checks malleability (returns false), type (returns false), challenge (returns false)
7. WebAuthn.verify accesses webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] - REVERTS if length < 33
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attacker crafts a UserOp where userOp.signature contains a SignatureWrapper with ownerIndex pointing to a passkey owner (64-byte) and signatureData containing ABI-encoded WebAuthnAuth struct with authenticatorData set to empty bytes or bytes shorter than 33. The abi.decode at CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol _validateSignature succeeds (WebAuthnAuth is a valid struct with bytes field). WebAuthn.verify proceeds through malleability check (if s is valid), type check (if type matches), challenge check (if challenge matches or not - these return false). But authenticatorData[32] at WebAuthn.sol line ~142 causes Panic revert due to out-of-bounds access. Note: if the type or challenge checks fail first, they return false gracefully. The revert only occurs if those checks pass but authenticatorData is short. An attacker can carefully construct clientDataJSON to pass both type and challenge checks while providing short authenticatorData.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. This is a code-level defect independent of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker targeting a wallet with a passkey owner crafts a UserOp with: (1) SignatureWrapper.ownerIndex pointing to the passkey owner, (2) signatureData containing a WebAuthnAuth struct with valid clientDataJSON (passing type and challenge checks via carefully crafted typeIndex, challengeIndex, and clientDataJSON content) but authenticatorData of length 0 to 32 bytes, and (3) valid-range r and s values (s <= P256_N_DIV_2). When validateUserOp processes this, it reaches authenticatorData[32] and reverts with Panic(0x32). The same applies to isValidSignature via ERC-1271. Note: constructing the scenario where type and challenge checks pass while authenticatorData is short requires the attacker to provide a valid clientDataJSON with correct type and challenge strings. The challenge check verifies against abi.encode(message) which the attacker knows (it's the userOpHash). This is feasible.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is a code defect where invalid WebAuthn signatures cause reverts instead of graceful failures. In ERC-4337 context, this changes the semantic from 'invalid signature' (return 1) to 'validation error' (revert). Bundlers that simulate UserOps off-chain would detect the revert during simulation and reject the UserOp, so on-chain gas waste is limited. For ERC-1271, callers expecting either 0x1626ba7e or 0xffffffff would instead receive a revert, potentially breaking integrating protocols. This is a defensive programming defect - all other validation paths in verify() return false, but this one reverts.
recommended_mitigations: Add a length check before accessing authenticatorData[32]: if (webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData.length < 37) return false; // WebAuthn spec requires minimum 37 bytes (32 rpIdHash + 1 flags + 4 signCounter)
Alternatively, at minimum: if (webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData.length < 33) return false;
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CoinbaseSmartWallet with a passkey owner (64-byte public key). 2. Craft a WebAuthnAuth struct with valid clientDataJSON (containing correct type and challenge fields) but authenticatorData = bytes('') (empty). 3. ABI-encode this into a SignatureWrapper with the passkey ownerIndex. 4. Call validateUserOp or isValidSignature with this signature. 5. Observe Panic(0x32) revert instead of return value 1 or 0xffffffff.
uncertainties: Whether an attacker can practically construct a clientDataJSON that passes both type and challenge keccak256 checks while keeping authenticatorData short - this requires knowing the expected challenge (userOpHash), which is publicly computable.
Whether bundlers in practice handle revert vs return-1 differently in their ban/throttle logic for accounts.
additional_testing_needed: Test WebAuthn.verify with authenticatorData of length 0, 1, 16, 32 to confirm revert behavior
Test full validateUserOp flow with short authenticatorData to confirm revert propagation
Test isValidSignature with short authenticatorData to confirm revert instead of 0xffffffff return
Verify that clientDataJSON can be constructed to pass type and challenge checks with a known challenge value
